Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a routine. I'd sometimes tell myself I'd study in the evenings after my extracurriculars, but it rarely happened that way. I'm more of an auditory learner, so I'd put on lecture recordings while doing other things, but I wouldn't call it organized. Most weeks I'd just drift through without a real plan, maybe cramming a bit when I remembered something was due.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go days without touching anything course-related, then suddenly realize a deadline was coming and panic a little. It wasn't really about the deadlines motivating me though—more like they'd sneak up on me because I wasn't paying attention. Some weeks I'd study more if I had nothing else going on, but that was rare with all my other activities.
Q3: Usually it was because I got an email reminder or someone in my group chat mentioned something about an assignment. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important, scroll around for a bit, and then close it without really doing much. The gamified task notifications actually got me to click in a few times, which was different from the usual boring prompts.
Q4: Not really, no. There were maybe a couple of weeks early on where I tried to make it a habit, but it never stuck. The platform just felt like another thing I had to do, and I already had enough of those. I think if the content had been more engaging from the start, maybe it would've become routine, but I lost interest pretty quickly.
Q5: The video lectures were probably the most useful for me since I learn better by listening. I could play them while walking to practice or doing chores, which fit my lifestyle better than reading. The discussion posts were occasionally interesting when people actually had something real to say, but that was hit or miss.
Q6: I basically ignored most of the readings and the supplementary PDFs. They just sat there and I kept telling myself I'd get to them later. The detailed assignment rubrics I'd skim at best—reading through all that text felt exhausting and I figured I'd understand what was needed when I started working on it. Spoiler: I usually didn't.
Q7: If something felt like it would actually help me understand the material quickly without a lot of effort, I'd do it. The gamified tasks were kind of fun because they broke things into smaller pieces and gave me immediate feedback. Anything that felt like busywork or took forever to get through just wasn't worth it to me, even if it was supposedly important.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff basically didn't exist in my world. If it wasn't required, I'd tell myself I'd do it if I had time, knowing full well I never would. Required activities at least had some consequence attached, so I'd force myself through them eventually. The optional ones just felt like extra weight I didn't need.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download lecture audio and listen to it on my phone when I was out. I also had a study group that met in person sometimes, and we'd talk through concepts without any of us being logged into anything. I'd borrow a friend's notes too, which helped me more than reading the official materials ever did.
Q10: There were times I'd spend an hour talking through problems with a classmate at a café, working things out verbally because that's how I process information. None of that showed up anywhere. Or I'd listen to a downloaded lecture three times while commuting, but the system only knew I'd downloaded it once. My actual studying was pretty invisible.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a lecture or save a PDF, and then that was it for my LMS interaction with that material. I'd listen to the same recording multiple times offline, or I'd have notes from it that I'd review later. The system probably thought I barely touched those resources when really I'd used them quite a bit.
Q12: Around midterms I just kind of checked out mentally. I was dealing with a lot of anxiety about everything—not just school but life stuff—and even though I have pretty good resilience, I was just bored and couldn't make myself care. The material felt disconnected from anything I actually wanted to do, so engaging felt pointless.
Q13: My extracurriculars took up a lot of mental energy, and by the time I got to coursework I was usually too tired to really focus. When I was confused about something, I'd avoid it rather than push through. The LMS became this thing I'd open, feel overwhelmed by, and close again. Fatigue was definitely a factor in how little I actually did there.
Q14: Oh, constantly. I'd open videos and let them play while I scrolled my phone or zoned out completely. I'd have discussion threads open but just skim them without really absorbing anything. It looked like I was engaging, but mentally I was somewhere else entirely. My body was present but my brain had left the building.
Q15: I put off almost every assignment until the last possible moment, even when I knew it was hurting my grades. Part of it was anxiety—starting felt overwhelming, so I'd avoid it. Part of it was just not caring enough to prioritize it. I'd tell myself I worked better under pressure, but really I was just scared to start and fail.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to do anything. I'd ignore the LMS for days, then suddenly there'd be something due in 12 hours and I'd frantically try to pull something together. It wasn't healthy or effective, but without that pressure I probably wouldn't have submitted anything at all. The deadline was my only real motivator.
Q17: Feedback came so late sometimes that I'd already moved on mentally. When I did get it, if it was negative I'd feel bad for a minute and then try not to think about it. Positive feedback was nice but didn't really change my behavior. I think I was too disconnected from the course for feedback to make a real difference in how I engaged.
Q18: The structure was pretty standard—watch this, read that, submit this—and it didn't do anything to grab my attention. I needed something more dynamic or interactive to stay interested. The gamified tasks were a step in the right direction, but the overall structure felt like it was designed for people who were already motivated, which I definitely wasn't.
Q19: The lack of flexibility made it harder. Everything felt very linear and prescribed, which bored me. The gamified elements helped a bit because they added some variety and made things feel less like a chore. But the heavy reliance on reading and the dense text-based materials were a bad fit for how I learn. More audio and interactive content would've helped.
Q20: I think some students, maybe like me, log in and click around without really doing the work. You might watch videos, browse discussions, check deadlines—all that shows up as activity—but when it comes to actually sitting down and completing assignments, that's a whole different level of effort. Activity doesn't equal productivity.
Q21: That sounds like me, honestly. I didn't do much on the LMS but I still passed. I studied in ways that didn't show up—listening to lectures offline, talking with friends, cramming right before exams. The system saw low engagement, but I was doing enough outside of it to scrape by. My exam scores were decent even if my assignment completion wasn't.
Q22: Logs can show when you clicked and how long something was open, but they can't show if you were actually paying attention or learning anything. They miss all the offline studying, the conversations with classmates, the thinking you do while walking around. They capture presence but not understanding or real engagement.
Q23: To me, being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and wanting to understand it. It's not just showing up or clicking through materials—it's being mentally present and curious. I wasn't really engaged this semester, even when I was technically doing things. I was going through the motions without any real investment.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much anxiety I was dealing with underneath the surface, or how bored and disconnected I felt from the material. It wouldn't capture the studying I did outside the platform, or the fact that I have decent resilience and can pull things together at the last minute. My actual experience was way more complicated than any log could represent.
